location.origin: Death Cab for Cutie formed in 1997 at Western Washington University in Bellingham, Washington -- a college town ninety miles north of Seattle, perched between the Cascades and Bellingham Bay. Ben Gibbard began recording four-track demos under the name (borrowed from the Bonzo Dog Doo-Dah Band's 1967 song) while working as a photo intern at the local alt-weekly. Chris Walla (guitar/producer), Nick Harmer (bass), and Nathan Good (drums) filled out the lineup. The band migrated to Capitol Hill, Seattle, by 2000. Bellingham enters the Seattle 360 engine for the first time -- the northernmost origin point in the engine's geography.
location.formative: Death Cab emerged through the late-1990s PNW indie-rock ecosystem: Barsuk Records (Seattle, est. 1998, Josh Rosenfeld), KEXP rotation, Capitol Hill venues (Crocodile Cafe, Neumo's, Showbox), Pitchfork coverage, and the college-radio circuit connecting Bellingham (KUGS) to Seattle (KEXP). Chris Walla's production shaped the sound from lo-fi four-track to full-studio architecture. The O.C. television placement (2003-2004) brought Death Cab to a national audience. Transatlanticism (2003) achieved gold certification. The major-label signing to Atlantic Records (2005) represents the canonical indie-to-major transition of the 2000s. Capitol Hill became the band's permanent base -- Gibbard has lived there for over two decades.
location.career: Something About Airplanes (1998, Barsuk Records -- debut, four-track lo-fi, Bellingham dorm recordings). We Have the Facts and We're Voting Yes (2000, Barsuk Records -- McGerr joins, KEXP rotation, Capitol Hill migration). The Photo Album (2001, Barsuk Records -- first national attention, Pitchfork reviews, synths enter). Transatlanticism (2003, Barsuk Records -- gold certification 500K, Pitchfork 8.0, The O.C. placement, the masterwork). Plans (2005, Atlantic Records -- major-label debut, platinum certification 1M+, Grammy nomination, Billboard Hot 100, 'I Will Follow You into the Dark'). Narrow Stairs (2008, Atlantic Records -- #1 Billboard 200, darker and angular). Codes and Keys (2011, Atlantic Records -- synth-heavy, Walla's last album). Kintsugi (2015, Atlantic Records -- Grammy nomination Best Rock Album, Depper and Rae join). Thank You for Today (2018, Atlantic Records -- gentrification themes, Capitol Hill changing). Asphalt Meadows (2022, Atlantic Records -- tenth album, twenty-five years). Also: The Postal Service with Jimmy Tamborello (Give Up, 2003, Sub Pop -- platinum, 'Such Great Heights'). Ben Gibbard solo (Former Lives, 2012, Barsuk Records). Ten Death Cab studio albums. Arena headliner. Two Grammy nominations. KEXP Anchor institution.

labels: Barsuk Records (1998-2004: Something About Airplanes, We Have the Facts, The Photo Album, Transatlanticism, You Can Play These Songs with Chords -- Seattle-based independent, est. 1998, Josh Rosenfeld; Death Cab is the flagship artist; also home to Laura Gibson degree 57 and The Long Winters degree 59)

Atlantic Records (2005-present: Plans, Narrow Stairs, Codes and Keys, Kintsugi, Thank You for Today, Asphalt Meadows -- major label; the indie-to-major transition; platinum sales, Grammy nominations, #1 Billboard 200)

members.name: Ben Gibbard
members.note: Founding member. The voice of the ache -- emotional precision, geographic specificity, the yearning quality that defines the catalog. Also The Postal Service (with Jimmy Tamborello), solo albums. Capitol Hill resident for 20+ years. Marathon runner. Environmental activist. The public face of Seattle indie rock.

members.name: Nick Harmer
members.note: Founding member. The consistent rhythmic anchor across all ten albums and every lineup change. Bellingham through Asphalt Meadows.

members.name: Jason McGerr
members.note: Joined 2000 (replacing Nathan Good). The rhythm section that carried the band from indie to arenas. Twenty-two years and counting.

members.name: Dave Depper
members.note: Joined after Chris Walla's departure. Portland musician (also Loch Lomond, Fruit Bats). Fills the guitar-production gap with a different sensibility -- less architectural, more textural.

members.name: Zac Rae
members.note: Joined alongside Depper for the Kintsugi era. Session musician and producer. Brings keyboard sophistication to the post-Walla lineup.

former_members.name: Chris Walla
former_members.note: Co-founding member and the band's in-house producer for the first seven albums. Walla's production defined the Death Cab sound: layered guitars, atmospheric keyboards, meticulous arrangements. Also produced records for The Decemberists, Tegan and Sara, Nada Surf. His departure after Kintsugi sessions is the most significant lineup change in the band's history.

former_members.name: Nathan Good
former_members.note: Original drummer. Played on Something About Airplanes and early recordings. Replaced by Jason McGerr.

former_members.name: Michael Schorr
former_members.note: Brief tenure between Good and McGerr during the transition period.

associated_musicians.name: The Postal Service / Jimmy Tamborello
associated_musicians.note: Gibbard's electronic side project with Jimmy Tamborello (Dntel). Give Up (2003, Sub Pop) achieved platinum certification. 'Such Great Heights' became one of the most ubiquitous indie songs of the 2000s. The Postal Service operates at E=5 on a single album. 2013 reunion tour. The project demonstrates Gibbard's ability to generate mass-audience connection across genres.

associated_musicians.name: Laura Gibson
associated_musicians.note: Gibson (E=2, Folk) and Death Cab (E=4, Indie Rock) share Barsuk Records. The same label aesthetic -- literary, emotionally precise -- at fundamentally different scales. Consecutive degrees (57-58) on one label. The manuscript page (57) yields to the ache (58).

associated_musicians.name: The Long Winters / John Roderick
associated_musicians.note: The Long Winters at degree 59. Barsuk labelmate. Roderick and Gibbard share the Seattle indie-rock social graph. Roderick produced Shelby Earl's debut (degree 55). The Barsuk trio: Gibson (57), Death Cab (58), Long Winters (59) -- three consecutive degrees on one label.

associated_musicians.name: Shelby Earl
associated_musicians.note: Earl (degree 55, Singer-Songwriter, E=2). John Roderick (Long Winters, Barsuk) produced Earl's debut. The social graph connects Earl to Death Cab through shared Barsuk infrastructure. Four consecutive degrees (55-56-57-58) connected through the Barsuk web.

associated_musicians.name: Jenny Lewis
associated_musicians.note: Rilo Kiley vocalist. Contributed vocals to Death Cab recordings. Part of the 2000s indie-rock social graph connecting Los Angeles and Pacific Northwest scenes.

associated_musicians.name: Modest Mouse
associated_musicians.note: The twin pillars of 2000s PNW indie rock. Both formed in Washington (Modest Mouse in Issaquah, Death Cab in Bellingham). Both signed to major labels after indie breakthroughs. Both debuted at #1 on the Billboard 200. Different sonic signatures, parallel commercial trajectories.

associated_musicians.name: Fleet Foxes
associated_musicians.note: Fleet Foxes (E=3, Indie Folk) and Death Cab (E=4, Indie Rock) represent two registers of PNW indie music. Both Pitchfork darlings. Both Sub Pop adjacent (Postal Service on Sub Pop; Fleet Foxes on Sub Pop). Cathedral harmony (53) and the ache (58) -- different acoustic elements, shared regional identity.

associated_musicians.name: Brandi Carlile
associated_musicians.note: Carlile (E=4, Folk/Rock) and Death Cab (E=4, Indie Rock) are both PNW arena headliners. Different paths to the same scale. Both fill the Gorge Amphitheatre. Both Grammy-nominated. The testimony roar (52) and the ache (58).

theory_nodes.concept: The ache (fifty-ninth acoustic element -- the acoustic quality of emotional precision under indie-rock instrumentation, where the vocalist conveys the exact distance between where you are and where you want to be; Gibbard's yearning vocal delivery makes longing structural rather than decorative; not whispered intuition but measured distance; not atmospheric dissolution but geographic specificity of absence)
examples: Death Cab for Cutie (degree 58) -- Gibbard's vocal delivery across ten albums returns to the same emotional territory: the precise measurement of distance. 'I need you so much closer' (Transatlanticism) is not a generic plea but a spatial claim. 'I will follow you into the dark' (Plans) measures the ultimate distance. The ache is the acoustic signature of longing rendered with geographic specificity.

Contrast with the murmur (degree 45, Damien Jurado) -- whispered, intuitive intimacy. The ache is louder, more precise, more architectural. Both measure distance; the murmur whispers it, the ache declares it.

Contrast with the manuscript page (degree 57, Laura Gibson) -- literary precision in folk form. The ache is emotional precision in rock form. Both foreground precision; different registers, different volumes.

Contrast with the cathedral harmony (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- choral immersion where the individual voice dissolves. The ache is the individual voice exposed -- one singer's distance, one singer's longing.
Contrast with the testimony roar (degree 52, Brandi Carlile) -- voice breaking through to arena scale. The ache reaches arena scale too but through vulnerability rather than power.
The ache breaks the thirteen-element folk-acoustic taxonomy (45-57) and begins a new acoustic register. Element 59 is the first post-folk-zone acoustic element.
theory_nodes.description: The ache is the fifty-ninth acoustic element in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the acoustic quality produced when emotional precision operates under indie-rock instrumentation -- the yearning quality in Gibbard's vocals that makes you feel the distance between where you are and where you want to be. The ache is not sadness; it is the precise measurement of longing. Ten albums document this quality across twenty-five years. The element breaks the thirteen-element folk-acoustic taxonomy (45-57) and opens a new acoustic register.

ancestors: The manuscript page (degree 57, Laura Gibson) -- literary precision in folk form. The ache is emotional precision in rock form.
The murmur (degree 45, Damien Jurado) -- whispered distance. The ache declares distance at volume.
The cathedral harmony (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- choral immersion. The ache is singular-voice exposure.
The testimony roar (degree 52, Brandi Carlile) -- powerful vocal breakthrough. The ache reaches the same scale through vulnerability.
genealogy.relationship: Fifty-nine acoustic elements across fifty-nine degrees. The ache is the first post-folk-zone element, breaking the thirteen-element folk taxonomy. Track element 60 at degree 59.

theory_nodes.concept: The Bellingham Ascent (fifty-ninth artist-city pattern -- the trajectory from college-town formation to arena-headliner scale where the origin city's identity becomes permanent acoustic signature regardless of commercial growth; Bellingham enters the engine as the northernmost origin point; the four-track dorm-room sound evolves into platinum production but the ache remains rooted in the college town's rainy specificity)
examples: Death Cab for Cutie (degree 58) -- formed at Western Washington University in Bellingham (1997), migrated to Capitol Hill Seattle (2000), signed to Atlantic Records (2005), debuted at #1 Billboard 200 (2008). The trajectory spans from four-track dorm recordings to platinum arena headliner. But the band still sounds like Bellingham: rainy, specific, yearning for distance in a place where distance is measured in mountain passes and ferry crossings.

Contrast with the writer-musician (degree 57, Laura Gibson) -- MFA-trained dual practice sustained by Portland's literary infrastructure. The Bellingham Ascent is geographic ascent: college town to arena, the origin as permanent identity.
Contrast with the prodigy return (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- departure and return. The Bellingham Ascent does not return -- it ascends while carrying the origin.

Contrast with the rural breakout (degree 52, Brandi Carlile) -- rural origin to city scale. The Bellingham Ascent is college-town origin to arena scale, with the college town as acoustic identity rather than biographical detail.
Bellingham as engine geography: the northernmost point. Beyond Seattle, beyond Everett, past the Skagit Valley. The engine's geographic reach extends ninety miles north.
theory_nodes.description: The Bellingham Ascent is the fifty-ninth artist-city pattern in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the trajectory from college-town formation to arena-headliner scale where the origin city's identity becomes permanent acoustic signature. Death Cab formed at WWU in Bellingham, migrated to Capitol Hill, signed to a major label, sold a million records, debuted at #1 -- and still sounds like a band from a rainy college town. Bellingham enters the engine as the northernmost origin point.

ancestors: The writer-musician (degree 57, Laura Gibson) -- dual-disciplinary practice. The Bellingham Ascent is geographic-trajectory practice.
The prodigy return (degree 53, Fleet Foxes) -- departure and return. The Bellingham Ascent is departure without return -- ascent carrying origin.
The rural breakout (degree 52, Brandi Carlile) -- rural to city. The Bellingham Ascent is college-town to arena.
genealogy.relationship: Fifty-nine artist-city patterns across fifty-nine degrees. The Bellingham Ascent introduces college-town-to-arena trajectory analysis. Track pattern 60 at degree 59.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- the indie-to-major crossing (fifty-eighth deployment; the blue note that sounds when an indie band signs to a major label and the audience splits; Death Cab's move from Barsuk to Atlantic is the canonical indie-to-major crossing of the 2000s; the gap between Transatlanticism's indie peak and Plans' platinum debut; same band, different infrastructure, different audience, different expectations)
examples: Death Cab for Cutie (degree 58) -- Transatlanticism (2003, Barsuk Records, gold certification) to Plans (2005, Atlantic Records, platinum certification). The same band, two years apart, different labels, different commercial worlds. The blue note plays in the gap: the fans who felt betrayed by the major-label signing, the fans who followed, the new fans who arrived via 'I Will Follow You into the Dark' and never knew Barsuk existed.
Contrast with the salvaged lyric (degree 57, Laura Gibson) -- creative survival after destruction. The indie-to-major crossing is commercial survival after growth -- not loss but transition.
Contrast with the borrowed landscape (degree 56, Lemolo) -- geographic displacement. The indie-to-major crossing is infrastructure displacement -- same city, different label ecosystem.

The indie-to-major crossing is the first blue note deployment outside the folk-adjacent zone since degree 44. The thirteen consecutive folk-adjacent blue notes (45-57) are broken along with the genre streak.

The crossing is not a betrayal -- it is the natural consequence of Barsuk's artist-development model succeeding. Barsuk built Death Cab to the point where Barsuk could not contain them. The blue note is structural, not moral.
theory_nodes.description: The indie-to-major crossing is the fifty-eighth blue note deployment in the Seattle 360 engine. It describes the blue note that sounds when an indie band signs to a major label and the audience splits between those who feel betrayed, those who follow, and those who arrive new. Death Cab's Barsuk-to-Atlantic transition (2003-2005) is the canonical example of this crossing in the 2000s. The blue note breaks the thirteen consecutive folk-adjacent deployments (45-57) along with the genre streak.

ancestors: Blue note -- the salvaged lyric (degree 57, Laura Gibson): creative survival. The indie-to-major crossing is commercial transition.
Blue note -- the borrowed landscape (degree 56, Lemolo): geographic displacement. The crossing is infrastructure displacement.
All fifty-seven prior blue note deployments (degrees 0-57)
genealogy.relationship: Fifty-eight deployments across fifty-nine degrees. Breaks the thirteen consecutive folk-adjacent deployments (45-57). First non-folk-adjacent blue note since degree 44. Track deployment 59 at degree 59.

theory_nodes.concept: Folk-adjacent streak broken at thirteen (Genre Stage 40 -- Indie Rock; BREAKS the thirteen-degree folk-adjacent run at degrees 45-57; Indie Rock is not folk-adjacent; electric guitars, full-band arrangements, arena-scale production, synthesizers; the engine pivots from the quiet register to the mid-frequency range)
examples: Death Cab for Cutie (degree 58) -- Indie Rock. Electric guitars, Chris Walla's layered production, synthesizers, arena-scale arrangements. Not folk-adjacent by any classification system. AllMusic: Indie Rock, Alternative Rock. The folk-adjacent streak of thirteen consecutive degrees (45-57) ends definitively.

The thirteen-degree folk-adjacent run: Folk (45), Folk (46), Indie Folk (47), Folk (48), Folk Punk (49), Indie Folk (50), Indie Folk (51), Folk/Rock (52), Indie Folk (53), Folk (54), Singer-Songwriter (55), Indie Folk (56), Folk (57). THIRTEEN degrees. Seven distinct sub-genre classifications. Now broken.
Genre Stage 39 was Folk at degree 57. Genre Stage 40 is Indie Rock at degree 58. The engine shifts genre families for the first time since degree 44.
The break is clean: Death Cab is unambiguously Indie Rock. There is no boundary case, no 'folk-adjacent with electric guitars' argument. The streak ends at thirteen.
theory_nodes.description: Indie Rock at degree 58 establishes Genre Stage 40, breaking the thirteen-degree folk-adjacent run (45-57). The streak ends definitively -- Death Cab is unambiguously Indie Rock, not folk-adjacent. The engine pivots from the quiet register to the mid-frequency range. Thirteen consecutive folk-adjacent degrees remains the longest genre-family streak in engine history.

ancestors: Folk at degree 57 (Laura Gibson) -- Genre Stage 39. The thirteenth and final folk-adjacent degree.
Twelve consecutive folk-adjacent at degrees 45-56 -- Genre Stages 27-37. Extended to thirteen at 57. Broken at 58.
Swallow quintet -- original record for longest same-family run. BROKEN at six (degree 50). Extended through thirteen. Now the record stands at thirteen.
genealogy.relationship: Genre Stage 40 at degree 58. Indie Rock breaks the folk-adjacent streak. Thirteen consecutive folk-adjacent (45-57) remains the all-time record. Track genre evolution at degree 59.

theory_nodes.concept: E=4 rises to fourteen deployments -- BREAKS E=2 three-peat (fourteenth E=4 deployment; energy distribution 2:19:13:14:10:2; E=4 passes E=3 for the first time since they tied; degrees 55-56-57 E=2 three-peat broken by the largest energy jump in recent engine history: E=2 to E=4)
examples: Death Cab for Cutie (degree 58) -- E=4. Platinum certification (Plans, 1M+). #1 Billboard 200 (Narrow Stairs). Two Grammy nominations. Arena headliner. KEXP anchor institution. The Postal Service at E=5 (different project). Death Cab's E=4 is definitive: major-label distribution, sustained national touring at 5,000-15,000 capacity, Billboard chart positions, Grammy visibility.
E=4 passes E=3 for the first time: 14 E=4 deployments versus 13 E=3. Distribution after degree 58: E1:2, E2:19, E3:13, E4:14, E5:10, E6:2.

The E=2 three-peat (55-56-57) is shattered: Earl (E=2), Lemolo (E=2), Gibson (E=2), Death Cab (E=4). The jump from E=2 to E=4 is the largest energy increase across consecutive degrees in recent engine history.
E=2 still leads with 19 deployments. But E=4 now stands alone in second place at 14, passing E=3 at 13.
theory_nodes.description: E=4 at degree 58 is the fourteenth E=4 deployment in the Seattle 360 engine. Energy distribution after degree 58: E1:2, E2:19, E3:13, E4:14, E5:10, E6:2. E=4 passes E=3 for the first time (14 vs 13). The E=2 three-peat (55-56-57) is broken by the largest energy jump in recent engine history (E=2 to E=4). Death Cab is definitively E=4: platinum certification, #1 Billboard 200, Grammy nominations, arena headliner.

ancestors: E=2 at degree 57 (Laura Gibson): three consecutive E=2 (55-56-57). Broken by E=4 at 58.
E=4 at degree 52 (Brandi Carlile): previous E=4. Carlile and Death Cab are both PNW arena headliners at E=4.
E=4 at degree 53 (Fleet Foxes): E=3, not E=4. Death Cab operates one energy level above Fleet Foxes.
genealogy.relationship: Fourteen E=4 deployments across fifty-nine degrees. E=4 passes E=3 for first time (14 vs 13). E=2 three-peat broken. Track at degree 59.

theory_nodes.concept: The Barsuk trio -- degree 2 of 3 (Gibson 57, Death Cab 58, Long Winters 59); the flagship act in the middle position; first three-degree label cluster; energy span E=2 to E=4 within one label; the indie-to-major transition occurs mid-cluster)
examples: Death Cab for Cutie (degree 58) -- Barsuk's flagship artist, position 2 of 3 in the label cluster. Gibson (57, E=2, Folk) provides the quiet literary entry point. Death Cab (58, E=4, Indie Rock) provides the commercial peak. Long Winters (59, E=3) will complete the cluster.

The energy span within the Barsuk cluster: E=2 (Gibson), E=4 (Death Cab), E=3 (Long Winters projected). One label, three energy levels. The label aesthetic is consistent (literary, emotionally precise) but the scale varies dramatically.
Death Cab's position in the cluster is unique: the only Barsuk artist to transition to a major label. Gibson and Long Winters stayed indie. The indie-to-major crossing occurs mid-cluster.
The three-degree Barsuk cluster is the first label cluster in the engine. Track whether any future label achieves three consecutive degrees.
theory_nodes.description: Death Cab for Cutie at degree 58 occupies the middle position in the Barsuk trio (57-58-59). As the flagship act, Death Cab represents the commercial peak of Barsuk's catalog -- the artist the label developed from four-track recordings to gold certification before the major-label transition. The trio spans E=2 to E=4 within one label's aesthetic framework.

ancestors: Barsuk bridge at degree 57 (Laura Gibson) -- Portland artist on Seattle label. Death Cab IS the Seattle label's identity.
Barsuk Records founding (1998) -- Death Cab's debut album was among the earliest Barsuk releases.
genealogy.relationship: Barsuk trio position 2 of 3 at degree 58. Flagship act. The indie-to-major crossing occurs mid-cluster. Track completion at degree 59 (Long Winters).

theory_nodes.concept: Bellingham enters the engine -- Western Washington University, college-town origin, northernmost geographic point; extends engine geography ninety miles north of Seattle beyond Everett and the Skagit Valley to the last major city before the Canadian border
examples: Death Cab for Cutie (degree 58) -- formed at Western Washington University in Bellingham, WA (1997). Bellingham is the engine's northernmost origin point. Prior degrees rooted in Seattle, Portland, Olympia, or the I-5 corridor's established nodes. Death Cab extends the geographic reach ninety miles north.

Bellingham's music infrastructure: KUGS (college radio), local venues, WWU campus culture. The college-town ecosystem that produced Death Cab is distinct from Seattle's professional infrastructure -- smaller, more DIY, more dependent on the university's cultural gravity.

The Bellingham-to-Seattle migration: Death Cab formed in Bellingham, relocated to Capitol Hill by 2000. The migration pattern is common for Pacific Northwest bands: form in a college town, move to Seattle for professional infrastructure.
Geographic expansion: the engine now spans from Portland (57, Gibson) to Bellingham (58, Death Cab) -- the full I-5 corridor from Oregon to the Canadian border.
theory_nodes.description: Bellingham enters the Seattle 360 engine at degree 58 as the northernmost origin point. Death Cab formed at Western Washington University in 1997, extending the engine's geography ninety miles north of Seattle. The Bellingham-to-Seattle migration (college town to Capitol Hill) represents a common PNW band trajectory. The engine now spans the full I-5 corridor from Portland to the Canadian border.

ancestors: Portland as artist base (degree 57, Laura Gibson) -- first Portland-based artist. Bellingham is the first Bellingham-origin artist.
Olympia entries (multiple degrees) -- college-town origin via Evergreen State. Bellingham is college-town origin via WWU.
genealogy.relationship: Bellingham enters at degree 58. Northernmost engine point. Geographic reach: Portland to Bellingham (full I-5 corridor). Track whether more Bellingham-origin artists appear.

theory_nodes.concept: Gender: male band returns -- first all-male act since degree 54 (Ivan & Alyosha); non-male three-peat (55-56-57) broken; overall ratio drops to 17:42 (~28.8%); folk zone non-male majority (7/13, 53.8%) remains as historical record of that zone
examples: Death Cab for Cutie (degree 58) -- all-male band (Gibbard, Harmer, McGerr, Depper, Rae). First all-male act since Ivan & Alyosha at degree 54. The non-male three-peat (55-56-57: Earl, Lemolo, Gibson) is broken.
Gender ratio after degree 58: 17:42 (non-male:male), ~28.8%. Drops from 29.3% at degree 57.
The folk zone's non-male majority (7/13, 53.8%) is now a historical record of that zone -- it ended with the zone at degree 57.
The engine returns to its modal pattern: predominantly male artists with periodic non-male entries.
theory_nodes.description: Death Cab for Cutie at degree 58 is an all-male band, breaking the non-male three-peat (55-56-57) and returning the engine to its modal male-predominant pattern. Gender ratio drops to 17:42 (~28.8%). The folk zone's non-male majority (7/13, 53.8%) stands as a historical record of degrees 45-57.

ancestors: Gibson (degree 57) -- seventeenth non-male. Three consecutive non-male (55-56-57). Broken at 58.
Ivan & Alyosha (degree 54) -- last all-male act before the non-male three-peat.
genealogy.relationship: Gender ratio 17:42 (~28.8%) at degree 58. Non-male three-peat broken. Folk zone non-male majority is historical record. Track at degree 59.

seattle_connection: THE ACHE -- Death Cab for Cutie (degree 58) is the most significant indie-rock band to emerge from the Pacific Northwest in the 2000s. Formed at WESTERN WASHINGTON UNIVERSITY in BELLINGHAM, WASHINGTON (1997) -- the engine's northernmost origin point. Migrated to CAPITOL HILL, SEATTLE by 2000. BEN GIBBARD (vocals/guitar), NICK HARMER (bass), JASON MCGERR (drums), DAVE DEPPER (guitar/keys, 2015-present), ZAC RAE (keys, 2015-present). Former: CHRIS WALLA (guitar/producer, 1997-2014). Ten studio albums across twenty-five years. SOMETHING ABOUT AIRPLANES (1998, BARSUK RECORDS -- debut, four-track lo-fi, Bellingham dorms). WE HAVE THE FACTS (2000, Barsuk -- McGerr joins, KEXP rotation, Capitol Hill migration). THE PHOTO ALBUM (2001, Barsuk -- Pitchfork reviews, synths enter). TRANSATLANTICISM (2003, Barsuk -- GOLD CERTIFICATION 500K, PITCHFORK 8.0, THE O.C. placement, the masterwork). PLANS (2005, ATLANTIC RECORDS -- major-label debut, PLATINUM CERTIFICATION 1M+, GRAMMY NOMINATION, BILLBOARD HOT 100, 'I Will Follow You into the Dark'). NARROW STAIRS (2008, Atlantic -- #1 BILLBOARD 200). KINTSUGI (2015, Atlantic -- GRAMMY NOMINATION Best Rock Album). ASPHALT MEADOWS (2022, Atlantic -- tenth album). THE POSTAL SERVICE with JIMMY TAMBORELLO (Give Up, 2003, SUB POP -- PLATINUM, 'Such Great Heights'). BARSUK TRIO: degrees 57-58-59 (Gibson, Death Cab, Long Winters) -- Death Cab is the flagship act, position 2 of 3. FOLK-ADJACENT STREAK BROKEN at thirteen (45-57). INDIE ROCK enters. Genre Stage 40. E=4 (14th deployment). Energy distribution 2:19:13:14:10:2. E=4 PASSES E=3 for first time (14 vs 13). E=2 THREE-PEAT BROKEN (55-56-57 to E=4 at 58). THE ACHE as 59th acoustic element -- emotional precision under indie-rock instrumentation, the yearning quality of Gibbard's vocals, the distance between where you are and where you want to be. THE BELLINGHAM ASCENT as 59th artist-city pattern -- college-town formation to arena-headliner scale, geographic origin as permanent acoustic identity. THE INDIE-TO-MAJOR CROSSING as 58th blue note -- Barsuk to Atlantic, the canonical indie-to-major transition of the 2000s, the audience that splits. BELLINGHAM enters the engine -- northernmost origin point. Gender 17:42 (~28.8%). Non-male three-peat broken. The quiet register ends. The volume rises. The ache fills arenas.

theory_genealogy: The ache (NEW at degree 58): 59th acoustic element. Emotional precision under indie-rock instrumentation. Breaks the thirteen-element folk-acoustic taxonomy. First post-folk-zone element. Track element 60 at degree 59.
The Bellingham Ascent (NEW at degree 58): 59th artist-city pattern. College-town-to-arena trajectory. Geographic origin as permanent acoustic identity. Track pattern 60 at degree 59.

Blue note -- the indie-to-major crossing (NEW at degree 58): 58th deployment. Barsuk to Atlantic. The audience splits. Breaks thirteen consecutive folk-adjacent deployments. Track deployment 59 at degree 59.
Folk-adjacent streak BROKEN at thirteen (at degree 58): Genre Stage 40 (Indie Rock). Thirteen consecutive (45-57) is the all-time record. Indie Rock enters.
E=4 rises to 14 (at degree 58): 14th deployment. Distribution 2:19:13:14:10:2. E=4 passes E=3 (14 vs 13). E=2 three-peat broken.
The Barsuk trio position 2 of 3 (at degree 58): Death Cab is flagship act. E=2 to E=4 within one label. Track completion at degree 59.
Bellingham enters the engine (NEW at degree 58): Northernmost origin. WWU. College-town-to-Seattle migration. Track.
Gender: 17:42 ratio (~28.8%). Non-male three-peat broken. Male band returns. Track at degree 59.
engine_sequencing: E=4 at 58: Distribution 2:19:13:14:10:2. 14th E=4 deployment. E=4 passes E=3. E=2 three-peat broken. Track at 59.
GENRE Stage 40 at degree 58: Indie Rock. BREAKS thirteen consecutive folk-adjacent (45-57). The record stands at thirteen. Track genre at 59.
BARSUK TRIO at degrees 57-58-59: Gibson (Folk E=2), Death Cab (Indie Rock E=4). Track completion at degree 59 (Long Winters).
GENDER at degree 58: 17:42 ratio (~28.8%). Non-male three-peat broken. Track at 59.

cultural_patterns: Artist-city taxonomy: FIFTY-NINE named patterns (adding The Bellingham Ascent at degree 58). College-town-to-arena analysis. Track.
Energy spectrum: 2:19:13:14:10:2. E=4 passes E=3 (14 vs 13). E=2 still leads at 19. E=2 three-peat broken. Track.
Genre evolution: Stage 40 (Indie Rock). Folk-adjacent streak BROKEN at thirteen (45-57). Record stands. Track.
BARSUK RECORDS: Gibson (57), Death Cab (58), Long Winters (59). Three-degree cluster continues. Death Cab is flagship act. Track.
BELLINGHAM, WASHINGTON: Northernmost engine origin. WWU. College town. KUGS radio. Track.
CAPITOL HILL: Death Cab base for 20+ years. Gibbard resident. Gentrification themes in Thank You for Today. Track.
CHRIS WALLA: Co-founder, producer, departed 2014. Defined the Death Cab sound. Also produced Decemberists, Tegan and Sara. Track.
THE POSTAL SERVICE: Gibbard + Tamborello. Give Up (Sub Pop, 2003). Platinum. E=5 on one album. Track.
ATLANTIC RECORDS: Major label. Plans platinum. Narrow Stairs #1. The indie-to-major crossing. Track.
THE O.C. / SETH COHEN EFFECT: Television placement bringing indie music to mainstream audience. Transatlanticism. Track.
KEXP: Death Cab as anchor institution. Sessions spanning entire career. Track.
PITCHFORK: Transatlanticism 8.0. Multiple album reviews. Track.
GRAMMY NOMINATIONS: Plans (Best Alternative), Kintsugi (Best Rock Album). Track.
MODEST MOUSE (degree 34): Twin pillars of 2000s PNW indie rock. Parallel trajectories. Both #1 Billboard 200. Track.
INDIE-TO-MAJOR TRANSITION: Barsuk to Atlantic (2003-2005). Canonical example. Track.
